Learning Python from a JavaScript Angle (31): So What Do You Want to Learn Next?
Yesterday I finally crossed the finish line of the Ironman contest. So today is day 31, and I'm going to use this last one as a little wrap-up chat with you about how the whole thing went.
Learning Python from a JavaScript Angle
Honestly, this series was originally only planned out to day 29, with day 30 being the wrap-up reflection. Sadly, I didn't pull that off.

Okay, fine — the real reason is that the contest happened to land right in my busiest stretch. And Python is a language I hadn't touched in a really long time, at least three years, so writing this near the end was genuinely painful. I'd actually started easing into it back in June, but with everything piling up I came close to quitting more than once. And every time I wanted to give up, there'd be a familiar Buzz Lightyear whispering in my ear:
I'll be the first to admit this whole "
" series isn't great — there are probably plenty of clunky sentences and other rough edges. I have to confess my grasp of Python is honestly pretty shallow.

But going through this — actually participating and sharing — made me notice a lot of spots where Python is genuinely nice and handy, and where it dodges a bunch of JavaScript landmines. If you've read the whole series, I think you've probably felt that too.
Still not coming to learn Python?

That doesn't mean Python has zero landmines, though. Every language has its own special traps. It's just that this series only ever covered tiny examples and tiny little exercises, so you basically never hit the truly terrifying ones. But for real, those still scare the heck out of me.
To wrap up, I want to leave a few words for anyone who read this series. No matter what, I hope everyone holds onto a

when it comes to programming languages. Because a language is, in the end, just a tool — and tools get phased out and replaced, same as jobs. No job lets you coast forever (and if one does, please tell me, I'd like to sign up first). You're just using this tool, this job, to survive and to keep your own life going.

So how do you avoid getting phased out and replaced? Only people who keep learning and growing don't get left behind. But that doesn't mean dabbling in a bit of this and a bit of that. I mean keep learning until you get genuinely good at one thing — and once you're at that level, looking at other languages becomes easy, because everything starts to click. You might ask: how do I tell when I've reached that level? Honestly, you don't have to. The little pockets of time will let you know on their own. When you keep the habit of learning, you've quietly picked things up without even realizing it. You just haven't noticed yet.

I've rambled enough here, so I'm getting ready to put a bow on the "Learning Python from a JavaScript Angle" series. I hope it made things a little easier and friendlier for a front-end dev who wants to pick up Python. I won't promise it's all correct, but I hope it helped you.
And finally, a huge thank you to the readers who quietly hit Like along the way. I'm really glad you enjoyed the nonsense I wrote, Q@Q
And to my team — if I hadn't pushed through this and talked things over together with all of you, I might've given up ages ago.
What would you want to learn "from a JavaScript angle" in your next Ironman article?
This whole series is also mirrored over on my blog

Every single night I was rushing to polish a draft and push it out right at midnight. I genuinely think this Ironman contest went pretty well, especially since it was my first time doing the team challenge. When you hit a conceptual snag in something you're writing, having people to talk it through with is huge. So I'd really recommend grabbing a group and joining the Ironman contest together — though keep in mind that if one person fails, everyone fails (lol), so try not to be the bad apple.
Oh, and one more thing — a friend recently asked me how many memes I used this time around, so here's a rough count for you (very rough estimate below).

I finally crossed the finish line of the Ironman contest. So for day 31, the last one, I'm just going to sit down and chat with you about how the whole thing went — what I learned about Python coming from JavaScript, and why I think the real takeaway is to keep learning no matter what tool you pick up.
